label: Chest wall contusion without cardiopulmonary red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit an uncomplicated chest wall contusion.
found: Your clinician diagnosed an uncomplicated chest wall contusion. We did not find fracture or dislocation concern, infection, nerve or blood-flow problems, or another emergency today.
home: Rest the chest wall from activity that makes pain worse.
Use ice, heat, gentle motion, support, or activity limits only as your clinician recommended.
Return to normal activity gradually as symptoms improve.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Do not use medicine to push through worsening pain, weakness, numbness, fever, or swelling.
return: Fever, rapidly worsening swelling, severe pain, or feeling very ill.
New numbness, weakness, blue or pale skin, deformity, or inability to use the area.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, sports medicine, orthopedics, therapy, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated chest wall contusion.
No fracture/dislocation concern, deep infection, neurovascular compromise, high-energy trauma, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Fracture/dislocation concern, open wound, infection concern, neurovascular compromise, severe or rapidly worsening pain, high-energy trauma, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
must_not_miss: Compartment syndrome or evolving emergency pain pattern.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports musculoskeletal strain, bruise, or overuse framing, activity modification, clinician-directed pain control, and escalation for infection, severe pain, worsening function, or neurovascular symptoms. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated musculoskeletal injury with fracture/dislocation, infection, neurovascular, pregnancy/pediatric, high-energy trauma, and specialist-directed pathways absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of uncomplicated chest wall contusion

label: AC joint sprain with negative x-ray and no dislocation or neurovascular red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit an uncomplicated AC joint sprain.
found: Your clinician diagnosed an uncomplicated AC joint sprain. We did not find fracture or dislocation concern, infection, nerve or blood-flow problems, or another shoulder emergency today.
home: Rest the shoulder from activity that makes pain worse.
Use ice, heat, gentle motion, sling/support, or activity limits only as your clinician recommended.
Return to normal activity gradually as symptoms improve.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Do not use medicine to push through worsening pain, weakness, numbness, fever, or swelling.
return: Fever, rapidly worsening swelling, severe pain, or feeling very ill.
New numbness, weakness, blue or pale skin, deformity, or inability to use the area.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, sports medicine, orthopedics, therapy, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated AC joint sprain with x-ray performed and negative.
No fracture/dislocation concern, neurovascular compromise, tendon or ligament rupture concern, high-energy trauma, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Fracture/dislocation concern, open wound, neurovascular compromise, tendon or ligament rupture concern, severe or rapidly worsening pain, high-energy trauma, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
must_not_miss: High-grade AC separation requiring separate planning.
Occult fracture or evolving emergency pain pattern.

terms: clinician diagnosis ac joint sprain xray negative
discharge diagnosis ac joint sprain xray negative
ac joint sprain xray negative diagnosed

ac joint sprain xray negative diagnosed

label: Palm abrasion without tendon or infection red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a minor palm abrasion.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a minor palm abrasion. We did not find a deep wound, retained foreign body, bite wound, infection, or another wound emergency today.
home: Keep the area clean and dry as your clinician instructed.
Change dressings, wash gently, and protect the area only as your clinician recommended.
Avoid soaking, picking, or reopening the abrasion.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Use antibiotic ointment, tetanus care, or other wound treatment only if your clinician recommended it.
return: Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, worsening swelling, or worsening pain.
Numbness, weakness, trouble moving the area, bleeding that will not stop, or concern that something is still in the wound.
The wound opens, worsens, or does not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, wound care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of minor palm abrasion.

No repair-needed laceration, deep structure injury, retained foreign body, bite wound, dirty wound requiring separate pathway, infection, diabetic-foot risk when relevant, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Repair-needed laceration, tendon/nerve/blood-vessel injury, joint violation, open fracture, retained foreign body, bite wound, dirty wound, infection concern, neurovascular compromise, severe pain, delayed high-risk presentation, diabetic foot when relevant, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed wound plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports basic cuts and wound-care self-care, keeping wounds clean, clinician-directed tetanus/ointment decisions, and escalation for infection, bleeding, retained foreign body, or deep-structure concern. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed minor abrasions with no deep structure, foreign-body, bite, dirty wound, infection, pregnancy/pediatric, or specialist-directed pathway.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of palm abrasion
no deep structure injury or retained foreign body
